role_summary: Co-organizer of the Howard University Nonviolent Action Group (NAG); Freedom Rider on the Anniston and Montgomery-to-Jackson legs in May 1961; voter-registration worker in Baton Rouge.
ai_reading: Diamond's top semantic neighbors are Harold K. Brown (entry 55, count 111), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF lawyer), Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (entry 60, Freedom Rider), and Judge D'Army Bailey (entry 26). The cluster is unusually heterogeneous, mixing SCLC inner circle (Vivian), NAACP legal (Carter), Freedom Rides (Mulholland), and the BPP-adjacent Brown, rather than concentrating in his Howard NAG / SNCC affiliation. The embedding therefore reads his testimony as cross-faction, the closest cultural analog to the May 1961 Freedom Ride alliance that pulled together SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC in one operation. His axis positions are all near-zero (the largest is +0.34 toward Collective Discipline), placing him among the most centrist voices in the corpus and consistent with the cross-faction neighbor pattern. The Howard NAG identification that defines his cultural placement is one cluster among several the embedding does NOT foreground, a reading the cultural-historical record would not have predicted.
biographical_paragraph: Dion Diamond was president of Howard University's freshman class in 1959-60 when he and others organized the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the campus arm of SNCC that ran sit-ins in Virginia and North Carolina and that, in one stretch, desegregated an area of Alexandria, Virginia inside two weeks [src: 1]. He had been born February 7, 1941 in Petersburg, Virginia [src: 1]. In May 1961 he joined the Freedom Rides, traveling on the Anniston, Alabama leg on the 14th and on the Montgomery-to-Jackson leg on the 24th; over the course of that activism he was arrested some thirty times [src: 1]. After Howard he transferred to the University of Wisconsin-Madison in 1963 for history and sociology, then later took up race relations and sociology at Harvard [src: 1]. He also worked voter registration in Baton Rouge during the early 1960s [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 27 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: The May 1961 Freedom Rides Diamond participated in were organized by the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) under James Farmer's direction, with the first set of thirteen riders (six white, seven Black) leaving Washington, D.C. on May 4, 1961 in two integrated buses bound for New Orleans; the goal was testing the December 5, 1960 Boynton v. Virginia Supreme Court ruling that interstate-bus terminal segregation violated the Interstate Commerce Act's anti-discrimination provisions [src: 2]. Diamond joined the SNCC-organized continuation rides after the original CORE riders were stopped by the May 14, 1961 Anniston firebombing of the Greyhound bus and the parallel Trailways bus beatings; the May 17 Nashville student rider arrival in Birmingham (organized through Diane Nash's coordination from the Nashville SCLC office) sustained the campaign and brought Diamond and the Howard NAG cohort into the May 24 Montgomery-to-Jackson leg [src: 2]. By the end of the summer 1961 ride campaign, more than four hundred Freedom Riders had been arrested in Jackson, Mississippi alone; the Interstate Commerce Commission's November 1, 1961 ruling banning bus-terminal segregation was the federal-regulatory outcome the campaign produced [src: 2]. The Howard NAG Diamond co-organized was the institutional pipeline through which the Howard cohort (Stokely Carmichael, Cleveland Sellers, Courtland Cox, Phil Hutchings, Charles Cobb, Muriel Tillinghast, among others) entered SNCC's full-time staff cadre across 1961-1963; the cross-faction neighbor pattern the AI's-reading observation above traces emerges from Diamond's having been operationally adjacent to multiple sub-movements at the formative moment each was crystallizing [src: 3].

gallery.alt: Birmingham News front page coverage of the May 14, 1961 Anniston Freedom Rider attacks
gallery.caption: Theodore Gaffney's photograph of the Anniston Greyhound bus attack as it appeared on the front page of the Birmingham News, Sunday May 14, 1961. The bus had been firebombed by a Ku Klux Klan mob on U.S. Highway 78 outside Anniston; Diamond joined the SNCC-organized continuation rides after the original CORE riders were stopped by this attack and the parallel Trailways bus beating in Birmingham later the same day.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)
